Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: 7 gray slots (3 in red container top, 4 in green container bottom). 7 bottom colors to place. Need to find all bottom color positions.
- Plan: Inspect current bottom row to find where yellow and magenta are now.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 14 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The bottom row has changed:
- Magenta (M) at (57, 38) - still there
- Yellow (Y) at (57, 52) - still there
- 5 gray placeholders at cols 10, 17, 24, 31, 45 (where the used colors were)
So the used colors turned into gray placeholders at the bottom. The remaining colors (magenta and yellow) are still at their original positions.
But when I tried to select yellow at (58, 52), it was no_impact. Wait - the yellow is at (57, 52) not (58, 52). Let me check - I was clicking at row 58 but the actual color squares are at row 57.
Actually, looking at the center coordinates: the colors are at row 57, col 38 for magenta and row 57, col 52 for yellow. But the squares are 4x4, so they span rows 56-59. Clicking at row 58 should still hit them.
Hmm, but the earlier successful placements also used row 58. Let me check what happened.
Wait - the first batch executed 10 actions successfully. The 11th action was MOUSE(row=58, col=52) which was supposed to select yellow. But it was marked as no_impact. Yet the board shows yellow still at (57, 52). So it didn't get consumed.
Maybe the issue is that after 5 placements, the interaction changed. Or maybe I need to click at the exact center of the color square. Let me try clicking at row 57 instead of 58.
Actually, looking more carefully at the bottom row, the gray placeholders are at row 58 (center), and the colored squares are at row 57 (center). They're at different rows! This is strange.
Let me look at the boundaries:
- M: center=(57,38), pixels=16 → 4x4 square, so rows 55-58, cols 36-39
- Y: center=(57,52), pixels=16 → 4x4 square, so rows 55-58, cols 50-53
- g placeholders: center=(58,10), pixels=4 → 2x2 square, so rows 57-58, cols 9-10
So the colored squares are 4x4 (rows 55-58) and the gray placeholders are 2x2 (rows 57-58). Clicking at row 58 should still hit the 4x4 squares.
But wait - in the first successful batch, I used row 58 for all color selections. Let me check the exact coordinates I used:
- O: (58, 31) → worked
- p: (58, 17) → worked
- R: (58, 10) → worked
- b: (58, 45) → worked
- N: (58, 24) → worked
- Y: (58, 52) → didn't work (no_impact)
But the first 5 worked with row 58. So why didn't the 6th work? Maybe the issue is something else. Let me try clicking directly on the center of the yellow square at (57, 52).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model updated: Used colors become gray placeholders at bottom. Yellow and magenta still available at (57,52) and (57,38). Previous clicks at row 58 worked for first 5 but not for yellow. Try clicking at exact center row 57.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking at exact center of yellow square
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 57, 'col': 52},  # Select yellow at center
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 34},  # Place in slot
])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
